title: Predecessors

tex_statement.raw:
% Definition file for row def_3_9 -- "Predecessors".
% Auto-generated stub by scaffold/solving_current_row.py at row start. The
% manager agent (or a worker dispatched by it) fills the body below with the
% Lean-faithful definition statement(s). Stays close to the lecture notes.
%
% Compiles standalone via the `subfiles` package -- the parent main.tex in
% this folder provides the preamble.

\documentclass[main]{subfiles}

\begin{document}
% ref: def_3_9
% title: Predecessors
\def\rowref{def\_3\_9}
\phantomsection\label{def_3_9}

\begin{Def}[Predecessors]
   Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $<$ a total order of $J \cup V$.
   The set of \emph{predecessors} of $v$ in $G$ are:
   \[\Pred^G_<(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC.\]
   We also put:
   \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]
\end{Def}

\end{document}
tex_statement.html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $<$ a total order of $J \cup V$. The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ are: \[\Pred^G_<(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC.\] We also put: \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]

lean_explanation:
This row formalises the lecture notes' `Pred^G_<(v)` and `Pred^G_≤(v)` via two `Set α`-valued definitions. The ambient `α : Type*` is the polymorphic vertex carrier, matching the LN's silent convention that the graph lives over some unspecified type — later chapters use real-valued vertices, so `α` cannot be assumed decidable or finite. `G : CDMG α` is the graph; `r : α → α → Prop` is the strict-precedes relation the LN writes as $<$ (kept as a bare relation rather than a `LinearOrder` on a subtype, so callers can apply it to bare vertices). `v : α` is the focus vertex.

`predLt G r v` is defined as `{ w | w ∈ G ∧ r w v }`. This is Lean's set-builder sugar: `Set α` is definitionally `α → Prop`, and the braces produce the predicate `fun w => w ∈ G ∧ r w v`. The membership `w ∈ G` invokes the `Membership α (CDMG α)` instance defined in `CDMGNotation.lean`, which unfolds via `CDMG.mem_iff` to `w ∈ G.J ∪ G.V`; using the typeclass form keeps the Lean code character-for-character aligned with the LN's $w \in G$.

`predLe G r v` extends this by taking the set-theoretic union with the singleton `{v}`: `{ w | w ∈ G ∧ r w v } ∪ {v}`. This mirrors the LN's $\cup \{v\}$ literally — `v` is added unconditionally, even when `v ∉ G`. Two `@[simp]` membership lemmas accompany the definitions and unfold each set for downstream rewriting: `w ∈ G.predLt r v ↔ w ∈ G ∧ r w v` (by `Iff.rfl`, since `predLt` is set-builder sugar) and `w ∈ G.predLe r v ↔ (w ∈ G ∧ r w v) ∨ w = v` (by `simp` through `Set.mem_union` and `Set.mem_singleton_iff`). Both definitions live in the `Causality.CDMG` namespace and take no precondition on `r` — the LN's "total order of $J \cup V$" wording is documentational, not a hypothesis.
design_choices:
**The order parameter is a bare `r : α → α → Prop`, not a `LinearOrder` on `↑(G.J ∪ G.V)`.** The LN writes $w < v$ for bare vertices, and every downstream consumer (e.g. the id-algorithm chapter's "we have the topological order $v_1 < v_2 < v_3$") uses the same bare-vertex notation. Bundling the relation as a `LinearOrder` on the subtype `↑(G.J ∪ G.V)` would force every caller to inject their vertices through `Subtype.mk` before applying `<`, breaking the LN's notational idiom. Keeping `r` separate from `G` also matches how the LN re-uses `Pred` against substituted graphs — $\Pred^{G_{\doit(C^\cmpl)}}_<(v)$ is then trivially `predLt G' r v` for the new graph `G'`, with the same `r`.

**The codomain is `Set α`, not `Finset α`.** Downstream uses intersect predecessors with other subsets, union them across an index, and pass them as the index for products of co-domains and for families of random variables. The CDMG layer keeps `α` fully polymorphic with no `DecidableEq` or `Fintype` instance assumed, so a `Finset` formulation would either propagate `DecidableEq α` everywhere (incompatible with the real-valued vertices in later chapters) or demand a `Fintype (G.J ∪ G.V)` hypothesis that the LN does not impose.

**No `IsTopologicalOrder G r` precondition.** The LN's "let $<$ be a total order of $J \cup V$" is documentational; the set $\{w \in G \mid w < v\}$ is well-defined for any binary relation. Imposing an order hypothesis in the signature would force every caller to thread a proof witness through for the same underlying set, and would block using `predLt` in the *statement* of claim 3.2, where the existence of a topological order is the conclusion rather than a hypothesis.

**`predLe`'s body is `{ w | w ∈ G ∧ r w v } ∪ {v}`, not `{ w | w ∈ G ∧ (r w v ∨ w = v) }`.** The two forms differ exactly when `v ∉ G`: the chosen form still adds `v`, the alternative silently drops it. The LN writes $\cup \{v\}$ literally, so we follow the LN. Callers that have `v ∈ G` in context get the equivalent set via the `mem_predLe` simp lemma.

**Names `predLt` / `predLe`, not bare `Pred` or `Pred_lt`.** `Pred` alone collides with `Nat.pred` and `Order.Pred` in core Lean and Mathlib, generating disambiguation noise on every reference. The camelCase suffixes match Mathlib's `Set.image` / `Set.preimage` conventions and remain visibly the LN's $\Pred^G_<$ / $\Pred^G_\le$.
